finding.recommendation: Adopt a thin authoritative deep-loop-workflows hub SKILL.md with a command-first/mode-first router, and preserve the five old workflow subtrees as direct mode packets under deep-loop-workflows/<mode>/ (context, research, review, ai-council, improvement), not under modes/<mode>/; keep deep-loop-runtime untouched. The hub owns the single advisor skill_id, global invariants, alias mapping, collision handling, and per-mode permission gates, then delegates to each mode packet's existing router/resources so behavior remains mode-specific and path migration stays deterministic.
key_tradeoffs: Thin hub adds one extra delegation step but avoids a giant collision-prone SKILL.md
Direct mode packets preserve old subtree shapes and reduce path rewrite risk, but look less cosmetically unified than modes/<mode>/
Root allowed-tools may need to be a union, so per-mode permission gates must be explicit and tested
Old graph metadata cannot remain live verbatim because skill_id must collapse to deep-loop-workflows
risks: Generic keyword collisions such as benchmark, convergence, review, and audit can reduce routing precision unless the root router uses command/mode-first namespacing
Root tool union can broaden permissions unless command YAMLs and mode guards enforce the original per-mode tool contracts
Leaving nested old graph-metadata.json files with old skill_id values can confuse skill-advisor migration
Path rewrites in executable YAML commands and SOURCE-FILES tables are numerous enough that missed references can break byte-identical artifact parity
depends_on: Q-ADVISOR alias and skill graph migration
Q-GOV feature catalog and manual testing playbook migration
open_questions: Are nested mode packet SKILL.md filenames accepted by validators, or should they be renamed to mode.md after the move?
Are the node_modules directories under deep-ai-council and deep-improvement tracked source artifacts or generated caches?
